reasoning_model_1: The audio accurately reproduces the text content 'Are you sure you really didn't touch my things?' without errors. The fluency is excellent; the speaker maintains a natural pace with a coherent sentence flow. Regarding the core requirement (Cognitive State: Doubting tone), the model performs very well. From 0.0s to 1.5s, the speaker uses a rising intonation at the end and places specific stress on 'really' (0.5s-0.7s) and 'didn't' to convey skepticism. The delivery sounds grounded and realistically human, effectively capturing a suspicious and doubting cognitive state without sounding exaggerated.
reasoning_model_2: The model correctly speaks the text, although it inserts a significant pause (0.7s-1.1s) after 'Are you sure', effectively breaking the sentence into two distinct phrases ('Are you sure? ... You really didn't touch my things?'). While the text is accurate, this phrasing feels slightly less fluid than a single cohesive sentence. In terms of the core feature, the model adopts a highly expressive, almost exaggerated tone. The pitch on 'sure' (0.5s) rises dramatically. While this clearly conveys doubt, the extreme prosody and the long pause make the delivery feel slightly theatrical or 'acted' rather than naturally conversational compared to Model 1.
model_comparison: Both models successfully convey the required 'doubting' tone and speak the text accurately. Model 1 is superior in terms of naturalness. It delivers the line as a single, cohesive thought with realistic human prosody, effectively using stress and intonation to convey doubt without over-acting. Model 2, while very expressive, splits the sentence with a long pause and uses exaggerated pitch shifts that feel slightly more performative and less natural. Model 1 strikes a better balance between expressiveness and realistic conversational flow.
